exercises.title: Map the Data Flow
exercises.description: Given an input sentence, trace how it flows through each transformer layer
exercises.prompt:
For the sentence 'The cat sat on the mat', trace:
1. Tokenization output
2. Embedding dimensions
3. How positional encoding changes the first vs. last token
4. Output of attention (conceptually)
exercises.solution:
Tokenization: ['The', 'cat', 'sat', 'on', 'the', 'mat'] (6 tokens)
Embedding: Each token → 768-dim vector
Positional encoding: Position 0 gets [0, 1, 0, 1...], Position 5 gets different values
Attention: 'cat' likely pays high attention to 'The' (article) and 'mat' (what it's related to)

exercises.title: Attention Mechanics
exercises.description: Understand how attention creates connections between tokens
exercises.prompt:
In the sentence 'The bank executive resigned', explain why the 'bank' token should attend heavily to:
- 'executive'
- 'resigned'
But less to:
- 'The'
- punctuation
exercises.solution: 'bank' needs context to be disambiguated. 'executive' and 'resigned' strongly suggest financial institution (not river bank). 'The' is grammatical but less informative about meaning. Attending more to semantically relevant tokens helps the model understand context.

exercises.title: Why Parallel > Sequential
exercises.description: Calculate training speed differences between RNN and Transformer
exercises.prompt:
If a sequence has 1000 tokens:
- RNN: Must process sequentially (1000 steps)
- Transformer: Can process all in one step

Assuming each step takes 10ms:
- How long for RNN?
- How long for Transformer?
exercises.solution:
RNN: 1000 tokens × 10ms/token = 10,000ms (10 seconds)
Transformer: 1 step × 10ms/step = 10ms (all tokens processed in parallel)
Transformers are ~1000x faster! (This is why they scaled to billions of parameters)

exercises.title: Design Challenge: Positional Encoding
exercises.description: Think about what properties positional encoding needs
exercises.prompt:
Why can't we just use:
- Position 0 → [1, 0, 0, 0]
- Position 1 → [0, 1, 0, 0]
- Position 2 → [0, 0, 1, 0]

What happens with sequences longer than the encoding dimension?
exercises.solution: One-hot encoding only works for fixed-length sequences. After position n, you run out of dimensions. Sine/cosine waves have infinite period - they can encode any position while maintaining similarity structure (nearby positions stay nearby in the vector space).
